返回本批次

研究人员使用 AI 分析了 40 万条 Reddit 帖子，揭示了 GLP-1 的副作用

Researchers used AI to analyze 400k Reddit posts, revealing GLP-1 side effects

www.seas.upenn.edu 作者 giuliomagnifico 分数 24 评论 2 2026/04/11 19:41
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 通过使用 AI 分析超过 400,000 个 Reddit 帖子，宾夕法尼亚大学的研究人员发现了患者报告的与 GLP-1（流行的减肥和糖尿病药物索马鲁肽和替泽帕肽）相关的症状，这些症状可能无法在临床试验或监管文件中完全体现。这项发表在《自然健康》杂志上的新研究涵盖了近 70,000 名 Reddit 用户超过五年的帖子，并强调了值得进一步研究的两类主要症状：生殖症状，包括月经周期不规则，以及与温度相关的症状，例如寒战和潮热。 “我们发现的一些副作用，如恶心，是众所周知的，这表明该方法正在接收真实的信号，”宾夕法尼亚大学工程学院计算机和信息科学 (CIS) 研究副教授、该研究的资深作者 Sharath Chandra Guntuku 说。 “被低估的症状是患者自己自发发出的线索，临床医生可能会注意到它们。” “临床试验通常会确定药物最危险的副作用，”CIS 教授、该研究的合著者 Lyle Ungar 补充道。 “但他们可能无法找到患者最关心的症状；尽管社交媒体不一定具有代表性，但大量帖子可能反映了额外的担忧。”研究人员警告说，他们的发现并不是因果关系。 “我们不能说 GLP-1 实际上导致了这些症状，”该研究的第一作者、CIS 博士生 Neil Sehgal 指出，他是 Guntuku 和 Ungar 的顾问。 “但在我们的样本中，近 4% 的 Reddit 用户报告了月经不规律，在女性样本中这一比例甚至更高。我们认为这是一个值得调查的信号。”左起：Lyle Ungar，计算机与信息科学 (CIS) 教授； Neil Sehgal，CIS 博士生；以及 CIS 研究副教授 Sharath Chandra Guntuku。 （图片来源：Sylvia 张）研究社交媒体促进健康 2011 年，Ungar 参与了最早的一项工作，旨在挖掘在线用户创建的内容，以获取有关药物不良反应的信息。 “在线患者社区的运作方式很像邻里的小道消息，”Ungar 说。 “服用这些药物的人们会实时交换笔记，分享很少会出现在医生办公室就诊或官方报告中的经历。”此后的几年里，社交媒体的使用不断增长，使得来自这些平台的数据越来越有希望成为有关药物副作用的信息来源，尽管平台本身使获取数据变得更加困难。 （Guntuku 还发表了关于适应平台访问变化的策略的研究。）“临床试验是黄金标准，但从设计上来说，它们的速度很慢，”Guntuku 说。 “这并不能取代试验，但它可以进展得更快，当一种药物几乎在一夜之间从利基市场变成主流时，这种速度很重要。”利用人工智能分析社交媒体 到目前为止，这个过程中最具挑战性的部分（Guntuku 称之为“计算社交聆听”）一直是规模。由于用户描述症状的方式各不相同，因此将各个社交媒体帖子映射到临床医生用来描述症状的监管活动医学词典 (MedDRA) 中的语言所需的工作限制了这种方法可以处理的数据量。现在，像 GPT 或 Gemini 这样的大型语言模型已经能够以前所未有的规模对社交媒体帖子进行系统分析。 Sehgal 表示：“大型语言模型使得这种分析速度更快，并且标准化程度达到了以前难以实现的程度。” CIS 博士生、该论文的第一作者 Neil Sehgal 领导使用 AI 分析了近 50 万个 Reddit 帖子，发现了临床试验中未报告的潜在 GLP-1 副作用。 （图片来源：Sylvia 张） 未报告的症状 虽然研究人员研究的人群确实不具有代表性——Reddit 用户更年轻，更有可能是男性，而且大部分来自美国——但他们集体描述中描述的症状在很大程度上与索马鲁肽和替泽帕肽已知的副作用相符：研究中约 44% 的用户描述了至少一种副作用，最常见的是某种形式的胃肠道不适。值得注意的是，报告症状的用户比例很高，而这些症状可能无法完全反映在当前的药物标签或常规不良事件报告中。近 4% 报告副作用的用户描述了生殖症状，包括月经变化，如经间期出血、大量出血和周期不规则。其他人则报告了与温度相关的投诉，例如发冷、感觉寒冷、潮热和发烧样症状。此外，疲劳是 Reddit 用户中第二大最常见的投诉，尽管达到了在相对较少的临床试验中报告阈值。宾夕法尼亚大学体重和饮食失调中心的高级研究员、该研究的合著者杰娜·肖·特罗涅里 (Jena Shaw Tronieri) 表示：“这些药物被认为是通过作用于大脑中称为下丘脑的部分发挥作用，这有助于调节多种激素。” “这并不意味着药物一定会引起这些症状，但它可能表明月经变化和体温波动的报告值得更系统地研究。”未来方向 在短期内，研究人员希望他们的发现能够鼓励临床医生和研究人员更仔细地研究患者在网上讨论的副作用。 “它们显然在患者的脑海中，这是值得关注的，”塞加尔说。该团队还希望将工作扩展到 Reddit 和英语社区之外，以测试相同的模式是否出现在不同的平台和人群中。 Ungar 表示：“我们还不知道我们在 Reddit 上看到的内容是否反映了全球 GLP-1 用户的体验，或者是否是针对美国在 Reddit 上发帖的人所特有的。”最终，研究人员相信，这种快速的、人工智能辅助的社交媒体分析可能成为发现新兴药物和健康趋势早期预警信号的有用方法。对于在网上迅速流行的物质，尤其是那些在监管松散或不受监管的市场销售的物质，如注射肽，Reddit 和 TikTok 等平台上的患者讨论可能是了解用户实际体验的最早线索之一。 “这种方法的重点在于它可以快速行动，而这正是它最有价值的时候，”冈图库说。这项研究是在宾夕法尼亚大学工程与应用科学学院进行的。作者报告没有外部资助。 Tronieri 报告代表宾夕法尼亚大学从诺和诺德获得了研究者发起的资助，并从 Currax Pharmaceuticals, LLC 获得了咨询费。其他作者报告没有利益冲突。

Original Summary

  1. By using AI to analyze more than 400,000 Reddit posts, Penn researchers have identified patient-reported symptoms associated with GLP-1s, the popular weight-loss and diabetes drugs semaglutide and tirzepatide, that may not be fully captured in clinical trials or regulatory documents. The new study, published in Nature Health, covers more than half a decade of posts from nearly 70,000 Reddit users and highlights two main classes of symptoms that warrant further study: reproductive symptoms, including irregular menstrual cycles, and temperature-related complaints, such as chills and hot flashes. “Some of the side effects we found, like nausea, are well known, and that shows that the method is picking up a real signal,” says Sharath Chandra Guntuku, Research Associate Professor in Computer and Information Science (CIS) at Penn Engineering and the study’s senior author. “The underreported symptoms are leads that came from patients themselves, unprompted, and clinicians could potentially pay attention to them.” “Clinical trials generally identify the most dangerous side effects of drugs,” adds Lyle Ungar, Professor in CIS and a co-author on the study. “But they can fail to find what symptoms patients are most concerned about; even though social media is not necessarily representative, a large collection of posts may reflect additional concerns.” The researchers caution that their findings are not causal. “We can’t say that GLP-1s are actually causing these symptoms,” notes Neil Sehgal, the study’s first author and a doctoral student in CIS advised by Guntuku and Ungar. “But nearly 4% of the Reddit users in our sample reported menstrual irregularities, which would be even higher in a female-only sample. We think that’s a signal worth investigating.” From left: Lyle Ungar, Professor in Computer and Information Science (CIS); Neil Sehgal, doctoral student in CIS; and Sharath Chandra Guntuku, Research Associate Professor in CIS. (Credit: Sylvia Zhang) Studying Social Media for Health In 2011, Ungar participated in one of the earliest efforts to mine online, user-created content for information about drugs’ adverse effects. “Online patient communities work a lot like a neighborhood grapevine,” says Ungar. “People who are living with these medications are swapping notes with each other in real time, sharing experiences that rarely make it into a doctor’s office visit or an official report.” In the years since, social media use has only grown, making data from these platforms increasingly promising as a source of information about the side effects of medications, even as the platforms themselves have made accessing the data more difficult. (Guntuku has also published research on strategies for adapting to changes in platform access.) “Clinical trials are the gold standard, but by design, they are slow,” says Guntuku. “This is not a replacement for trials, but it can move much faster, and that speed matters when a drug goes from niche to mainstream almost overnight.” Leveraging AI to Analyze Social Media Until now, the most challenging part of this process, which Guntuku calls “computational social listening,” has been scale. Because users vary in how they describe their symptoms, the effort required to map individual social media posts to language in the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA), which clinicians use to describe symptoms, limited the amount of data this approach could handle. Now, large language models like GPT or Gemini have enabled the systematic analysis of social media posts at unprecedented scale. “Large language models have made it possible to do this kind of analysis much faster with a level of standardization that could be difficult to achieve before,” says Sehgal. Neil Sehgal, a doctoral student in CIS and the paper’s first author, led the analysis of nearly half a million Reddit posts using AI, which unearthed potential GLP-1 side effects not reported in clinical trials. (Credit: Sylvia Zhang) Unreported Symptoms While the population the researchers studied is admittedly not representative — Reddit users are younger, more likely to be male and disproportionately based in the United States — the symptoms described in their collective accounts largely match the known side effects of semaglutide and tirzepatide: about 44% of users in the study described at least one side effect, most commonly some form of gastrointestinal distress. What stood out was the nontrivial percentage of users who reported symptoms that may not be fully reflected in current drug labeling or routine adverse-event reporting. Nearly 4% of users who reported side effects described reproductive symptoms, including menstrual changes such as intermenstrual bleeding, heavy bleeding and irregular cycles. Others reported temperature-related complaints, such as chills, feeling cold, hot flashes and fever-like symptoms In addition, fatigue ranked as the second most common complaint among Reddit users, despite reaching reporting thresholds in relatively few clinical trials. “These drugs are thought to work by engaging part of the brain called the hypothalamus, which helps regulate a wide variety of hormones,” says Jena Shaw Tronieri, Senior Research Investigator at Penn’s Center for Weight and Eating Disorders and a co-author of the study. “That doesn’t mean the medications are necessarily causing these symptoms, but it could suggest that reports of menstrual changes and body temperature fluctuations are worth studying more systematically.” Future Directions In the near term, the researchers hope their findings will encourage clinicians and researchers to take a closer look at the side effects patients are discussing online. “They’re clearly on patients’ minds, and that’s worth paying attention to,” says Sehgal. The team also hopes to expand the work beyond Reddit and beyond English-language communities to test whether the same patterns appear across different platforms and populations. “We don’t really know yet whether what we’re seeing on Reddit reflects the experience of GLP-1 users globally, or whether it’s particular to the kind of person who posts on Reddit in the United States,” Ungar says. Ultimately, the researchers believe this kind of rapid, AI-assisted social media analysis could become a useful way to spot early warning signs around emerging drugs and wellness trends. For substances that trend quickly online, especially those sold in loosely regulated or unregulated markets, like injectable peptides, patient discussions on platforms like Reddit and TikTok may offer one of the earliest clues to what users are actually experiencing. “The whole point of this kind of approach is that it can move quickly, and that’s exactly when it’s most valuable,” says Guntuku. This study was conducted at the University of Pennsylvania School of Engineering and Applied Science. The authors report no outside funding. Tronieri reports receiving an investigator-initiated grant, on behalf of the University of Pennsylvania, from Novo Nordisk and receiving consulting fees from Currax Pharmaceuticals, LLC. The other authors report no conflicts of interest.

评论（默认中文）

nonameiguess 2026/04/11 21:35
耶稣基督，来吧。他们发现了哪些文本包可能来自或可能不是来自人们报道的，这些文本可能是或可能是对实际发生的任何事情的准确报道。如果真的发生了，没啥问题，中年女性担心自己体重超标时常用的一种药物与潮热和月经不调有关，这些事情都会发生在中年女性身上，这是完全自然的原因，无论她们是否服用药物。

这显然甚至没有尝试设立对照组。不服用此药的中年女性有哪些报道？ “计算社交聆听？”这是一个笑话吗？作为一名正教授，你是如何在与计算机科学相邻的机器学习方面走得这么远，并且显然不费心去学习实验方法和统计学的原始基础知识？

Jesus Christ, come on. They found what bundles of text that may or may not have come from people reported, which may or may have been an accurate reporting of anything that actually happened. And if it actually happened, no shit, a drug commonly used by middle-aged women concerned they're getting overweight is associated with hot flashes and menstrual irregularity, things that happen to middle-aged women, for completely natural reasons regardless of whether they're taking drugs or not.

This apparently does not even attempt to have a control group. What are middle-aged women who don't take this drug reporting? "Computational social listening?" Is this a joke? How do you get this far into machine learning adjacent computer science as a full professor and apparently not bother to learn the raw basics of experimental methods and statistics?

testfrequency 2026/04/12 00:45
“研究人员”

溜进去，溜出去

“Researchers”

Slop in, Slop out
